key_insight: Training-free dimensional adaptation plus carefully weighted averaging can merge heterogeneous billion-parameter LLMs surprisingly well, but only when interpolation ratios are kept far from balanced.
engineering_relevance: This provides a simple, low-cost baseline for model fusion that avoids distillation, adapters, and alignment machinery, making heterogeneous checkpoint merging more practical to test and deploy.
method: Expand the smaller model into the larger parameter space for union-style merging.
Truncate the larger model into the smaller parameter space for intersection-style merging.
Apply ratio-controlled interpolation to transfer complementary capabilities without training.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports experiments on Qwen-family model pairs across math reasoning, code, language understanding, commonsense, knowledge, and instruction-following benchmarks, where small-ratio interpolation often improved over strong source checkpoints.
watch_item: Near-balanced interpolation can collapse and task-level gains may come with regressions elsewhere, so ratio choice and capability trade-offs need careful validation.
paper_title: Rethinking Heterogeneous LLM Merging: A Weighted Model Averaging Perspective
